title: Management of Pituitary Hormone Replacement Through Transition From Adolescence to Young Adulthood
authors: Mehul T. Dattani, MBBS, DCH, FRCPCH, FRCP, MD

items.title: Why hypopituitarism requires lifelong vigilance
items.text: Deficits may evolve from isolated GH deficiency to combined pituitary hormone deficiency—regular testing is needed across childhood, adolescence, and adulthood.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "The condition may evolve throughout adolescence and childhood, and hence there is a need for vigilance and regular testing of pituitary function on a lifelong basis."

items.title: How to manage hypopituitarism at adolescent transition
items.text: Transition clinics with pediatric and adult endocrinologists plus nurse specialists address adherence, puberty/fertility concerns, high-risk behaviors, and re-evaluation of hormone needs.
items.reference: Management at Transition: "Transition clinics with input from pediatric/adolescent endocrinologists, adult endocrinologists, and the endocrinology clinical nurse specialist are critical to the successful transition of patients."

items.title: When childhood-onset GH deficiency does not need retesting
items.text: Retesting is not required with ≥2 pituitary hormone deficiencies, confirmed genetic cause, or structural defect (except isolated ectopic posterior pituitary)—25–88% may reverse isolated idiopathic GHD.
items.reference: Treatment: "retesting is not required for those with more than 2 pituitary hormone deficiencies, a confirmed genetic pathogenic variant accounting for the hypopituitarism, and/or a specific hypothalamic-pituitary structural defect on MRI with the exception of an ectopic posterior pituitary."

items.title: How to diagnose secondary hypothyroidism without TRH test
items.text: Low free T4 with inappropriately normal or low TSH is sufficient—TRH stimulation does not change management.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "a low concentration of free T₄ in the presence of an inappropriately low or normal concentration of TSH is sufficient for the diagnosis of secondary or tertiary hypothyroidism"

items.title: Why central diabetes insipidus may appear only after starting glucocorticoids
items.text: Cortisol sufficiency is required for renal free water clearance via inhibition of ADH—ACTH deficiency masks coexistent DI until hydrocortisone is started.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "ACTH deficiency may mask coexistent central diabetes insipidus until glucocorticoid replacement is initiated."

items.title: How to counsel adolescents on hydrocortisone timing and sick-day rules
items.text: Dose at least 3× daily; double/triple during illness; emergency IM hydrocortisone (25 mg <1 yr, 25–50 mg 1–5 yr, 100 mg >5 yr); discuss alcohol and late sleep schedules.
items.reference: Treatment: "Doses should be doubled or even tripled in the face of illness, with patient education on how and when to administer emergency intramuscular hydrocortisone"

items.title: Puberty in hypopituitarism: septo-optic dysplasia versus combined deficiency
items.text: Septo-optic dysplasia may have normal or precocious puberty; isolated combined pituitary hormone deficiency more often has delayed or absent puberty.
items.reference: Treatment: "Puberty may be precocious or occur at the normal time in those with hypopituitarism associated with septo-optic dysplasia, but it may be delayed or absent in those with isolated combined pituitary hormone deficiency."

items.title: MRI predictors of combined pituitary hormone deficiency in congenital hypopituitarism
items.text: Ectopic posterior pituitary carries 27-fold higher hypopituitarism risk; stalk agenesis and corpus callosum abnormalities predict combined deficiency over isolated GHD.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "the risk of hypopituitarism was 27 times greater in patients with an ectopic posterior pituitary."

items.title: Why PROP1 testing matters for suprasellar masses
items.text: PROP1 pathogenic variants can cause suprasellar masses that spontaneously resolve—genetic diagnosis may avoid unnecessary surgery.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "PROP1 pathogenic variants are associated with suprasellar masses that undergo spontaneous resolution, and the presence of a PROP1 pathogenic variant may help to reassure families of the absence of a brain tumor and circumvent the need for surgery."

items.title: How to start desmopressin safely in central DI
items.text: Titrate using paired plasma and urine osmolalities inpatient; err on underdosing—overdose causes hyponatremia and dangerous fluid shifts; warn about MDMA and overdrinking.
items.reference: Treatment: "Treatment should generally err on the side of underdosing, since overdosing DDAVP can result in water intoxication, hyponatremia, and rapid fluid shifts"

items.title: POU1F1 mutations cause GH, TSH, and prolactin deficiencies
items.text: Dominant-negative POU1F1 (e.g., p.R271W) affects somatotroph, thyrotroph, and lactotroph development—not ACTH or gonadotropins; MRI shows small anterior pituitary with normal posterior pituitary.
items.reference: Case 1: "Pathogenic variants are associated with GH, TSH, and prolactin deficiencies, but not ACTH or gonadotropin deficiencies."

items.title: Why fatigue in a teen with ectopic posterior pituitary needs full axis reassessment
items.text: Ectopic posterior pituitary strongly predicts evolving combined deficiency—reassess GH dose, cortisol, and thyroid when new symptoms appear at puberty.
items.reference: Case 2: "With an ectopic posterior pituitary, this patient would most likely have developed other hormone deficiencies."

items.question: A patient with GH, TSH, and prolactin deficiencies and small anterior pituitary most likely has a variant in:
items.explanation: POU1F1 (PIT1) affects GH, TSH, PRL—not ACTH or gonadotropins.
items.reference: Case 1: "Answer: D)POU1F1"

items.question: A 15-year-old girl with ectopic posterior pituitary and fatigue on GH therapy. Best next steps include:
options: Assess GH dose, cortisol, and thyroid function and evaluate comorbidities
items.explanation: Answer E (B, C, D)—optimize GH, test axes, evaluate associated conditions.
items.reference: Case 2: "Answer: E) B, C, and D"

items.question: GH retesting at transition is NOT required if the patient has:
options: Ectopic posterior pituitary only as structural defect
Single hormone deficiency and normal MRI
items.explanation: Genetic confirmation or ≥2 deficiencies or structural defect (except ectopic PP alone) exempts retesting.
items.reference: Treatment: "retesting is not required for those with more than 2 pituitary hormone deficiencies, a confirmed genetic pathogenic variant"

items.question: Levothyroxine dosing in central hypothyroidism should be guided by:
options: TSH target in lower normal range
Free T4 maintained in upper half of normal range
items.explanation: TSH is unreliable; free T4 guides therapy, especially with hypothalamic obesity risk.
items.reference: Treatment: "Free T₄ concentrations should be maintained in the upper half of the normal range"

items.question: Emergency IM hydrocortisone dose for a child older than 5 years is:
items.explanation: Age-stratified emergency dosing: 100 mg for >5 years.
items.reference: Treatment: "emergency intramuscular hydrocortisone (doses <1 year, 25 mg; 1-5 years, 25-50 mg; >5 years, 100 mg)"

items.question: Testing for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism is most informative:
options: During mini-puberty or pubertal ages (>8 girls, >9 boys)
items.explanation: HP-gonadal axis is quiescent outside mini-puberty and puberty.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "Testing for hypogonadotropic hypogonadism is not useful outside of the mini-puberty (up to age 6 months) and pubertal phases (boys aged >9 years, girls aged >8 years)"

items.question: In idiopathic childhood isolated GHD followed ≥3.5 years, additional pituitary deficiencies developed in:
items.explanation: GeNeSIS data: 5.5% developed additional deficiencies in long follow-up.
items.reference: Evolution of Endocrinopathies: "Combined pituitary hormone deficiency developed in 2.0% of the overall cohort and in 5.5% among children followed for a minimum of 3.5 years."

items.question: In female hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, preferred estrogen replacement emphasizes:
options: 17β-estradiol with transdermal route favored for bone accrual
items.explanation: Transdermal 17β-estradiol may be superior for bone and uterine growth with lower genotoxic metabolites.
items.reference: Treatment: "It has been suggested that the transdermal route is superior to the oral route in terms of bone accrual and uterine growth."

items.question: Loss to follow-up after transition most risks:
items.explanation: Nonadherence risks adrenal crisis, hypothyroidism, low BMD, fatigue, low libido.
items.reference: Management at Transition: "Loss to follow-up by a physician may be associated with the risk of adrenal crisis, a hypothyroid state, reduced bone mineral density, and fatigue with reduced libido."

items.question: Reported incidence of congenital hypopituitarism is approximately:
items.explanation: Congenital hypopituitarism is rare but not ultra-rare.
items.reference: Significance of the Clinical Problem: "Congenital hypopituitarism is a rare disorder and may be congenital (1 in 4000 to 1 in 10,000 live births)"

items.question: In craniopharyngioma, early weight gain may:
options: Precede diagnosis of suprasellar mass by months and predict hypothalamic obesity
items.explanation: Weight/BMI changes may precede mass diagnosis and predict hypothalamic obesity.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "in patients with craniopharyngiomas, early changes in weight and BMI have been shown to precede the diagnosis of a suprasellar mass by several months and may be predictive of future hypothalamic obesity."

items.question: GH replacement in childhood tumor survivors:
options: At replacement doses is not associated with increased recurrence; second neoplasm risk unclear
Always causes tumor recurrence within 1 year
Requires doses higher than childhood growth doses lifelong
items.explanation: Replacement GH does not increase recurrence; second primary neoplasm signal is uncertain—use minimum effective dose.
items.reference: Treatment: "GH therapy in replacement dosages is not associated with an increased risk of recurrence or progression, although some evidence suggests that there may be a small increased risk of second primary neoplasms, but this is not completely clear."

items.question: Holistic hypopituitarism care at transition should include:
options: Ophthalmology, psychology, dietitian, and nurse specialist among others
items.explanation: Main conclusions list multidisciplinary team including ophthalmology, neurodevelopment, nursing, psychology, dietitian, social work.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "A holistic approach involves a pediatric and adult (at transition and thereafter) endocrinologist, ophthalmologist, neurodevelopmental pediatrician, clinical nurse specialist, psychologist, dietitian, and social worker."

items.statement: Pituitary hormone deficits may reverse even with structurally abnormal pituitary glands.
items.explanation: Main conclusions emphasize reversibility.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Reversibility of pituitary hormone deficits may occur, even in the presence of a structurally abnormal pituitary gland."

items.statement: Septo-optic dysplasia may paradoxically have early or normally timed puberty.
items.explanation: Pubertal timing is highly variable in hypopituitarism.
items.reference: Main Conclusions: "Paradoxically, in patients with septo-optic dysplasia, it may occur early."

items.statement: TRH stimulation is required to distinguish secondary from tertiary hypothyroidism in children.
items.explanation: Low free T4 with low/normal TSH suffices; TRH does not change management.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "an additional thyrotropin-releasing hormone stimulation test neither distinguishes the 2 nor changes clinical management."

items.statement: Organic isolated childhood GHD has higher risk of evolving to CPHD than idiopathic GHD.
items.explanation: 20.7% vs 5.5% in long follow-up subgroups.
items.reference: Evolution of Endocrinopathies: "there was a higher frequency of combined pituitary hormone deficiency (9.9% in the overall cohort, and 20.7% in the subgroup followed up for a minimum of 3.5 years)."

items.statement: Starting levothyroxine before ruling out ACTH deficiency can unmask central DI.
items.explanation: Glucocorticoid replacement unmasks ADH axis when cortisol was masking DI.
items.reference: Treatment: "Before commencing replacement, preexisting ACTH deficiency should be detected to avoid the risk of precipitating a hypocortisolemic crisis."

items.statement: Between 25% and 88% of patients may reverse GH secretion at transition retesting.
items.explanation: Stated in treatment section on retesting.
items.reference: Treatment: "Between 25% and 88% of patients reverse their GH secretion at the time of transition."

items.statement: MDMA use can be extremely dangerous in patients on desmopressin due to overdrinking risk.
items.explanation: Recreational overdrinking risk highlighted.
items.reference: Treatment: "certain "recreational" drugs such as MDMA ("Ecstasy") may lead to overdrinking, which could be extremely dangerous in patients on desmopressin."

items.statement: Adolescent adherence challenges are a major barrier to optimal hypopituitarism care.
items.explanation: Listed under barriers to optimal practice.
items.reference: Barriers to Optimal Practice: "Lack of engagement from the young person."

items.assertion: Assertion: Hypopituitarism requires lifelong pituitary function monitoring.
items.reason: Reason: Deficits may evolve from single to multiple hormone deficiencies over time.
items.explanation: Both true—evolution documented in GeNeSIS and congenital cohorts.
items.reference: Evolution of Endocrinopathies: "Children with isolated GH deficiency have a significant risk of developing additional pituitary deficiencies."

items.assertion: Assertion: Ectopic posterior pituitary on MRI increases risk of hypopituitarism 27-fold.
items.reason: Reason: Ectopic posterior pituitary is associated with isolated GHD only without other deficits.
items.explanation: Assertion true; ectopic PP predicts broader hypopituitarism risk.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "the risk of hypopituitarism was 27 times greater in patients with an ectopic posterior pituitary."

items.assertion: Assertion: Transition clinics improve outcomes in adolescent hypopituitarism.
items.reason: Reason: Transition clinics are critical for successful transfer to adult care.
items.explanation: Both true per management at transition section.
items.reference: Management at Transition: "Transition clinics with input from pediatric/adolescent endocrinologists, adult endocrinologists, and the endocrinology clinical nurse specialist are critical to the successful transition of patients."

items.assertion: Assertion: POU1F1 mutations cause GH, TSH, and prolactin deficiencies.
items.reason: Reason: POU1F1 mutations also cause ACTH and gonadotropin deficiencies.
items.explanation: Assertion true; ACTH and gonadotropins spared.
items.reference: Case 1: "Pathogenic variants are associated with GH, TSH, and prolactin deficiencies, but not ACTH or gonadotropin deficiencies."

items.assertion: Assertion: Low free T4 with low or normal TSH confirms central hypothyroidism.
items.reason: Reason: TRH stimulation is required before starting levothyroxine.
items.explanation: Assertion true; TRH not required.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "a low concentration of free T₄ in the presence of an inappropriately low or normal concentration of TSH is sufficient for the diagnosis"

items.assertion: Assertion: Delaying sex steroid replacement far beyond usual pubertal age is not generally recommended.
items.reason: Reason: Long-term bone mineral accrual benefits favor timely pubertal induction.
items.explanation: Both true—individualized but not excessively delayed.
items.reference: Treatment: "Delaying commencement of the latter beyond the usual pubertal age (~12 years in girls and 14-15 years in boys) is not generally recommended, given the long-term benefits on bone mineral accretion."

items.assertion: Assertion: DDAVP dosing should err on the side of underdosing.
items.reason: Reason: Overdosing causes water intoxication and dangerous rapid fluid shifts.
items.explanation: Both true—inpatient titration recommended.
items.reference: Treatment: "overdosing DDAVP can result in water intoxication, hyponatremia, and rapid fluid shifts that are difficult to correct safely"

items.assertion: Assertion: PROP1 variants may produce resolving suprasellar masses mimicking tumors.
items.reason: Reason: All PROP1 patients require immediate craniotomy regardless of genetics.
items.explanation: Assertion true; genetics may avoid unnecessary surgery—reason false.
items.reference: Diagnosis: "the presence of a PROP1 pathogenic variant may help to reassure families of the absence of a brain tumor and circumvent the need for surgery."
